title: Economic Bulletin Issue 8, 2025
summary: The ECB Governing Council kept its three key interest rates unchanged at its 18 December 2025 meeting, maintaining a data-dependent approach without pre-committing to a rate path. December 2025 staff projections show headline HICP inflation expected to average 2.1% in 2025, 1.9% in 2026, 1.8% in 2027, and 2.0% in 2028, with the 2026 projection revised up by 0.2 percentage points due to stronger-than-expected wage growth. GDP growth was revised up across the forecast horizon to 1.4% in 2025, 1.2% in 2026, and 1.4% in 2027-2028, driven by resilient domestic demand and improving external conditions.
key_points: ECB held rates unchanged on 18 December 2025; Governing Council reiterated commitment to 2% inflation target in medium term
Headline HICP inflation projected at 2.1% (2025), 1.9% (2026), 1.8% (2027), 2.0% (2028); 2026 forecast revised up 0.2pp due to wage growth
GDP growth revised up to 1.4% in 2025, 1.2% in 2026, and 1.4% in 2027-2028, compared with September 2025 projections
Unemployment at 6.4% in October 2025 near historical lows; job vacancy rate at lowest since COVID-19 pandemic
Services inflation rose to 3.5% in November 2025, while goods inflation declined to 0.5%; wage growth at 4.0% annual rate above September projections
Wage growth expected to ease to below 3% by end of 2026, with ETS2 implementation now postponed to 2028 with 0.2pp upward impact on headline inflation
why_it_matters: This ECB Economic Bulletin provides the official institutional assessment of euro area economic conditions and the ECB's policy outlook. The revised-up inflation and growth projections signal the ECB sees an economy that is more resilient than previously expected, but with persistent services inflation driven by wage pressures. This shapes market expectations for the pace of future rate cuts and provides context for the ECB's 'meeting-by-meeting' approach to monetary policy.
macro_context: The ECB projects euro area inflation returning to target (2.0%) by 2028, with the 2026 upward revision reflecting stickier services inflation and elevated wage growth. Domestic demand is the primary growth driver, supported by rising real wages and additional government spending on infrastructure and defence (notably in Germany). External factors include expected export recovery in 2026 amid declining trade policy uncertainty despite tariff impacts. The labour market remains robust with 6.4% unemployment near historical lows, but cooling labour demand is evident through lower vacancy rates.
